description: A suspected flaky test needs repeatable signal across reruns, isolation changes, or timing controls before changing code.
body: Apply when a test fails intermittently, times out, or depends on order, time, network, concurrency, random data, or global state. A single pass after rerun does not prove the implementation is correct; it only changes confidence. Execution action: capture failing and passing runs, note seed/order/time controls, isolate the test file or case, and record whether the failure follows shared state or timing. Do not label a deterministic failure as flaky just because it is inconvenient. Verification signals include repeated run counts, consistent stack or assertion location, order-dependent reproduction, and cleanup of leaked resources. Testing should provide the evidence; deep root-cause diagnosis belongs to code.debugging. Use when Code Testing work touches test runner output, fixture intent, coverage boundaries, flake history, and negative paths. Do not use this guidance to override a more specific pack, user instruction, or repository contract. Actions: use the fact to constrain the change and test plan; inspect the current files first, keep the change scoped to the owning boundary, and record what evidence changed the decision. Validation signals include focused test output, regression tests, fixture assertions, and rerun evidence. Risk boundary: escalate or stop when the task involves testing implementation details only, masking flakes, and claiming coverage without behavior proof, missing owner evidence, or live external state.
